summary: Weak keyword co-occurrence in auth guard; no traced installer harm
details: A a static rule fired on dist/guards/auth.guard.js citing co-occurrence of the tokens 'ping', 'GET', and 'id' within the file. These keywords in isolation are consistent with ordinary NestJS-style authentication guard code (health-check endpoints, HTTP verb strings, identifier fields) and do not, on their own, demonstrate exfiltration, remote code execution, credential theft, or an attacker-controlled network destination. No hardcoded attacker endpoint, no reads of installer secret paths (~/.aws, ~/.ssh, ~/.npmrc, browser stores), no lifecycle scripts fetching remote code, and no import-time side effects were identified. Semantic corroboration of malicious intent is absent, so the pattern match should be treated as low-signal until a human confirms or refutes intent.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
